title: DarkSword iOS Full-Chain Exploit Kit Deployed by State-Sponsored Actors and Spyware Vendors: CISA Adds Three Zero-Days to KEV as 220 Million iPhone and iPad Devices Face Complete Takeover Risk (CVE-2025-31277, CVE-2025-43510, CVE-2025-43520)

timeline.event: DarkSword first deployed by UNC6748 in watering hole attacks against Saudi Arabian targets using Snapchat-themed lure pages
PARS Defense (Turkish commercial surveillance vendor) begins deploying DarkSword against Turkish users
UNC6353 (suspected Russian state-sponsored group) begins using DarkSword against Ukrainian targets, transitioning from the Coruna exploit kit
PARS Defense launches second DarkSword campaign targeting Malaysian users
Google GTIG, iVerify, Lookout, and Zimperium begin coordinated deep analysis of the DarkSword exploit kit
Apple releases emergency patches: iOS 18.7.3, iOS 26.3, iOS 15.8.7, iOS 16.7.15 addressing all three zero-days in the DarkSword chain
Coordinated public disclosure by Google GTIG, iVerify, Lookout, and Zimperium; full technical details of DarkSword exploit chain published
Cloud Security Alliance publishes detailed research note on DarkSword full-chain exploitation by state actors
CISA adds CVE-2025-31277, CVE-2025-43510, and CVE-2025-43520 to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with April 3, 2026 federal patching deadline
DarkSword exploit code leaks publicly on GitHub, significantly increasing exploitation risk for unpatched devices

simple_summary.what_happened: Security researchers discovered a sophisticated 3-stage iPhone and iPad hacking kit called 'DarkSword' that chains three zero-day vulnerabilities together to completely take over any iOS device running versions 18.4 through 18.7. The attack begins when a victim visits a compromised website — no app download or phishing link needed. The kit was secretly deployed by state-sponsored hackers and a Turkish spyware company for months before discovery, targeting individuals in Saudi Arabia, Ukraine, Turkey, and Malaysia. On March 20, 2026, CISA added all three vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and ordered US federal agencies to patch by April 3, 2026.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any iPhone or iPad running iOS/iPadOS 18.4 through 18.7 is vulnerable — an estimated 220-270 million devices worldwide. This also affects macOS Sequoia 15.x, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS running unpatched versions. High-value targets include government employees, journalists, activists, executives, and anyone using an iPhone for sensitive communications. Organizations allowing personal iOS devices on corporate networks face risk of credential theft and lateral movement.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker compromises a legitimate website or creates a convincing fake site and embeds a hidden 1x1 pixel iframe containing the DarkSword payload. Step 2: When the victim visits the website in Safari, CVE-2025-31277 triggers a buffer overflow in JavaScriptCore (WebKit), giving the attacker arbitrary read/write access within the Safari browser process. Step 3: Using CVE-2025-43510, the attacker exploits improper kernel lock-state checking to escape the Safari sandbox via the GPU process, gaining access to system memory shared between processes. Step 4: CVE-2025-43520 is triggered to corrupt kernel memory through the mediaplaybackd service, granting the attacker complete kernel-level write access and bypassing Apple's Page Protection Layer (PPL) and Secure Page Table Monitor (SPTM). Step 5: The attacker deploys a persistent spyware payload (GHOSTBLADE or GHOSTKNIFE) that silently exfiltrates photos, messages, location history, cryptocurrency wallet data, Safari browsing history, installed apps, and device credentials — sending everything to attacker-controlled servers.
what_you_should_do: Immediately update all iPhones and iPads to iOS 18.7.3 or later (iOS 26.3 for newer devices; iOS 18.7.6 for iPhone XR/XS/XS Max; iOS 15.8.7 for iPhone 6s and earlier; iOS 16.7.15 for iPhone 7/7 Plus)
Block known malicious domain cdncounter[.]net and all subdomains at your enterprise DNS/firewall layer
Enforce Mobile Device Management (MDM) policies requiring minimum iOS version updates for all corporate-connected devices
Review browser logs and endpoint telemetry for unexpected JavaScript loading, WebKit crashes, or mediaplaybackd abnormalities
If compromise is suspected: perform full forensic analysis, rotate all credentials from the affected device, and notify affected individuals of potential data exposure
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: DarkSword represents one of the most sophisticated mobile threats seen in years — a complete 3-stage zero-day chain that compromises iPhones silently through a webpage visit alone, with no user interaction beyond browsing. The fact that it was deployed for over 4 months before discovery, leveraged by both nation-state actors and a commercial surveillance vendor, and now has its code publicly leaked means the risk to unpatched devices is immediate and severe. At 220+ million vulnerable devices, this is a mass-scale threat with potential for espionage, credential theft, and corporate network intrusion via personal devices.

tactics.description: Victims are compromised by simply visiting a legitimate website that has been injected with a hidden iframe containing the DarkSword JavaScript payload — no phishing link click or app download required
CVE-2025-31277 exploits a buffer overflow in Apple's JavaScriptCore (WebKit) engine to achieve arbitrary code execution within the Safari browser process context

CVE-2025-43510 exploits improper kernel lock-state checking to escape the WebContent sandbox via the GPU process, then CVE-2025-43520 exploits a memory corruption bug in mediaplaybackd to achieve full kernel write primitives and bypass Apple PPL and SPTM mitigations

DarkSword payloads are entirely JavaScript-based and heavily obfuscated, bypassing Code Page Layout Randomization (CPLR) and native application sandboxing controls. Malicious iframes are 1x1 pixel in size and rendered off-screen to avoid visual detection

Post-exploitation spyware payloads (GHOSTBLADE, GHOSTKNIFE) exfiltrate photos, Safari history, messages, installed apps, location history, calendar data, notes, and cryptocurrency wallet keys from the compromised device
Stolen device data is transmitted to attacker-controlled command-and-control infrastructure, with cdncounter[.]net observed as a primary C2 domain in DarkSword campaigns

detection_guidance.summary: Detection of DarkSword exploitation is challenging due to its entirely JavaScript-based and heavily obfuscated delivery mechanism. Primary detection vectors include network-level monitoring for C2 domain communication, mobile EDR analysis for WebKit/kernel crash patterns, and MDM-enforced version compliance checks. After public exploit code release, automated scanning by opportunistic attackers is expected to increase significantly.

log_sources.source: Mobile Device Management (MDM) / Enterprise Mobility Management
log_sources.description: Monitor device OS version compliance. Any iOS device below 18.7.3 (or equivalent patched version for the device model) should be flagged immediately as high-risk.

Monitor for DNS queries and HTTP(S) connections to cdncounter[.]net and all subdomains (static.cdncounter[.]net, cdn.cdncounter[.]net). Any resolution of this domain indicates active exploitation attempt or successful compromise.

Look for unexpected crash reports from WebKit (com.apple.WebKit.WebContent), GPU process (com.apple.WebKit.GPU), or mediaplaybackd. These processes crashing in sequence during passive web browsing is a strong indicator of exploitation attempt.
Monitor for unusual outbound HTTPS traffic patterns from iOS devices, particularly large data transfers to unfamiliar CDN endpoints following web browsing sessions.

what_to_look_for.indicator: DNS query to cdncounter[.]net or subdomains
what_to_look_for.description: Primary C2/delivery domain confirmed in DarkSword campaigns by Google GTIG and iVerify. Any DNS resolution of this domain indicates active DarkSword involvement.

what_to_look_for.indicator: Hidden iframe with near-zero dimensions in web page source
what_to_look_for.description: DarkSword delivery mechanism uses 1x1 pixel or invisible iframes to load exploit payloads. Web proxy or browser content inspection may detect this pattern.
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.response_body CONTAINS '<iframe' AND (height='1' OR width='1' OR style='display:none')

what_to_look_for.description: The DarkSword exploit chain typically causes sequential process crashes as it escalates through Safari sandbox to kernel. Crash sequences on these three processes during browsing are highly suspicious.

what_to_look_for.indicator: iOS device running version below 18.7.3 accessing sensitive enterprise resources
what_to_look_for.description: Unpatched iOS devices that have network access to corporate resources pose immediate risk. Any iOS device below the patched version threshold should be treated as potentially compromised.

immediate_actions.description: Update all Apple devices immediately. Federal agencies must comply by CISA deadline of April 3, 2026. For device-specific patches: iPhone XS/XR/XS Max requires iOS 18.7.6; iPhone 7/7 Plus requires iOS 16.7.15; iPhone 6s and earlier requires iOS 15.8.7; all newer iPhones/iPads should update to iOS 18.7.3 or iOS 26.3.

immediate_actions.action: Block DarkSword C2 Domain at Network Level
immediate_actions.description: Immediately add cdncounter[.]net and all subdomains (*.cdncounter.net) to DNS blocklists, firewall deny rules, and web proxy blacklists. This prevents both initial delivery and post-exploitation data exfiltration from DarkSword-infected devices.

immediate_actions.action: Enforce MDM Minimum iOS Version Policy
immediate_actions.description: Configure Mobile Device Management (MDM) to enforce minimum iOS version compliance. Devices running iOS below 18.7.3 (or applicable patched version for their model) should have corporate resource access restricted until updated.

immediate_actions.action: Audit and Triage High-Value Targets for Potential Compromise
immediate_actions.description: Prioritize forensic review of iOS devices belonging to executives, IT administrators, government personnel, journalists, and individuals with access to sensitive corporate systems. Look for crash logs, unusual network activity, and battery drain patterns consistent with spyware activity.

immediate_actions.action: Review Corporate BYOD Policy and Network Segmentation
immediate_actions.description: Ensure personal iOS devices connecting to corporate Wi-Fi, VPN, or enterprise applications are subject to MDM enrollment and OS version enforcement. Consider network segmentation to isolate unmanaged devices from critical internal resources.
